title: Ghee or oil: which to cook with
excerpt: Every Indian kitchen keeps a tin of ghee and a bottle of oil, but Ayurveda teaches that each fat has its own character, and choosing the wrong one for your body or the season quietly works against your health.
body_html:
the everyday question at the stove

Most of us reach for whatever is closest when the pan heats up. Ghee if it is a special dal, refined oil if it is everyday sabzi, coconut oil if the recipe says so. The choice feels almost random, and in modern nutrition it is often framed purely around saturated fat or smoke point. Ayurveda takes a different view: every fat carries its own guna (quality), acts on specific doshas, and behaves differently in different seasons. Getting this right is considered part of pathya, the daily regimen that keeps digestion steady and the body nourished.

The Sanskrit word for the broad category of fats and oils is sneha, which also means love or tenderness. That double meaning is deliberate. Ayurvedic physicians saw oleation, internal and external, as an act of care for the body, not merely a cooking technique.

what the classical texts say about each fat

Ghee (ghrita) holds a uniquely high place in the literature. Caraka calls it the best of all fats (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana), praises its ability to improve memory, intellect, digestion, and the quality of all seven dhatus, and says it is particularly suited to Pitta and Vata conditions. It is considered madhura (sweet) in taste, sheeta (cooling) in potency, and deepana in small quantities, meaning it kindles rather than smothers agni when used appropriately. Ashtanga Hridaya (Sutrasthana) similarly places ghee at the top of the sneha hierarchy and recommends it across seasons for those with a balanced or predominantly Pitta constitution.

Sesame oil (tila taila) is the runner-up, especially for Vata. Caraka Samhita (Sutrasthana) describes tila taila as heavy, hot in potency, and particularly penetrating, which makes it ideal for the dry, light, cold qualities that dominate in Vata imbalance. It is the classical choice for abhyanga, the daily oil massage, and for winter cooking in colder climates.

Mustard oil (sarshapa taila) comes up in Bhavaprakasha as pungent and heating, with a capacity to clear Kapha and support skin health. It is best suited to Kapha-dominant individuals or to cold, damp seasons when the body needs that extra warmth. It is considered less ideal for Pitta because its heat can aggravate that dosha.

Coconut oil (narikela taila) is acknowledged in the classical literature, including Bhavaprakasha, as cooling, light, and sweet. This makes it well-suited to Pitta and to the summer months when the body is already carrying heat. Its lightness means it is gentler on digestion than sesame or mustard, though Kapha individuals should use it more sparingly.

matching fat to dosha and season

The practical Ayurvedic approach is to think in two directions at once: your constitution (prakriti) and the current season (ritu). A Vata person in winter, for example, needs warming and unctuousness, so sesame oil or ghee suits them well. A Pitta person in June, when the sun is fierce, should lean toward ghee or coconut oil and reduce mustard. A Kapha person in the monsoon, when heaviness and dampness are already high, should choose lighter fats in smaller amounts and may benefit from mustard's penetrating heat.

Ashtanga Hridaya (Sutrasthana) offers a seasonal guideline: ghee is beneficial across most seasons for those who are otherwise healthy; heavier, hotter oils like sesame and mustard are more appropriate in hemanta and shishira (the cold and dewy seasons); and lighter, cooler choices are preferred in grishma and sharad (summer and autumn).

One practical note from the texts: Ayurveda does not recommend refined, industrially processed oils. Cold-pressed or traditionally prepared oils are the intended reference point throughout the classical literature. The process of refining strips away many of the qualities that the texts attribute to these fats.

when to check with a vaidya or doctor

This article is general educational information and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical advice. Individual body constitutions and health conditions vary considerably, and the right fat for you depends on your full health picture.

See a qualified Ayurvedic vaidya or your doctor if you have a known lipid disorder, liver or gallbladder disease, obesity, diabetes, or any chronic cardiovascular condition before significantly changing your fat intake. If you notice unusual digestive discomfort, heaviness after meals, skin flare-ups, or any symptoms that persist or worsen, seek professional evaluation rather than adjusting fats on your own.

Try This Today

  • Vata in winter: sesame or ghee: If you tend toward dryness, cold hands, or irregular digestion and it is the cold season, cook with cold-pressed sesame oil or ghee. Use a teaspoon or two, not a large quantity, so agni remains kindled rather than overwhelmed.
  • Pitta in summer: ghee or coconut: In the hot months, swap mustard or refined oils for ghee or cold-pressed coconut oil. Both have a cooling post-digestive effect that helps keep internal heat in check.
  • Kapha in monsoon: small amount of mustard: If you carry Kapha qualities, especially in the damp monsoon season, a small quantity of traditional cold-pressed mustard oil can add the warmth and penetration the body needs. Keep portions modest.
  • Avoid mixing fats unnecessarily: The classical texts treat each fat as a distinct medicine. Mixing multiple oils in one dish dilutes their specific qualities. Choose one fat per preparation.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Every Indian kitchen keeps a tin of ghee and a bottle of oil, but Ayurveda teaches that each fat has its own character, and choosing the wrong one for your body or the season quietly works against your health.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Ghee is ranked first among all fats in Caraka Samhita and suits Pitta and Vata in most seasons
Sesame oil is the classical choice for Vata and for winter, while mustard oil warms Kapha in cold or damp seasons
Coconut oil's cooling nature makes it best for Pitta and for summer months

glossary_terms.definition: The category of fats and oils; also means love or tenderness, reflecting Ayurveda's view of oleation as nourishing care
Clarified butter, or ghee; considered the finest fat in classical Ayurveda
Sesame oil; prized for its warming, penetrating quality and suitability for Vata
The wholesome daily regimen, including food choices, that maintains health
Quality or attribute, such as heavy/light or hot/cold, used to classify foods and medicines
Season; Ayurveda recognises six seasons and recommends adjusting diet and fats with each

sources: Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, chapters on dinacharya and seasonal regimen
Bhavaprakasha, Bhavaprakasha Nighantu, taila varga (section on oils and fats)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "sneha" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, sneha is defined as: The category of fats and oils; also means love or tenderness, reflecting Ayurveda's view of oleation as nourishing care

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to vata in winter: sesame or ghee. If you tend toward dryness, cold hands, or irregular digestion and it is the cold season, cook with cold-pressed sesame oil or ghee. Use a teaspoon or two, not a large quantity, so agni remains kindled rather than overwhelmed.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to pitta in summer: ghee or coconut. In the hot months, swap mustard or refined oils for ghee or cold-pressed coconut oil. Both have a cooling post-digestive effect that helps keep internal heat in check.
